verses.fhb_text: Blow a shofar in Zion! Sound an alarm on my holy mountain! Let all who live in the land tremble, for the day of our Father is coming, for it is near.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: First occurrence of "day of YHWH" in the chapter — Father is the named speaker of the army of judgment (cf. v.11).

Renderings:
• "day of our Father" — YHWH resolves to our Father per FHB-1.2-1 Resolution Order step 4; the Father is the speaker of vv.11-12 and the commander of the army
• "my holy mountain" — Father's voice through the prophet; "my" preserved as Father's first-person possessive
• Two exclamations — the shofar alarm and the call-to-tremble are explicit prophetic alarm cries, earning their place per FHB-9.15-1

Hebrew/Greek: yom YHWH (day of YHWH) → "day of our Father"
verses.footnote: The Day of YHWH that runs through Joel is the day our Father acts — his army moves at his voice, his judgment falls, his rescue follows. The prophets carry the same theme as our Father's decisive intervention in history.

lint_flags.detail: Footnote contains Hebrew/Greek term 'YHWH'. FHB footnotes are reader-only — no Hebrew/Greek transliterations (FHB-8.4 footnote style). Move technical terms to decision_notes.

verses.fhb_text: A day of darkness and gloom, a day of clouds and thick darkness. Like dawn spreading across the mountains, a great and mighty army comes — never has there been the like, nor will there be again, through the years of generation after generation.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Locust army dual imagery preserved per FHB-5.11-1.

Renderings:
• "a great and mighty army comes — never has there been the like" — em dash marks the shift from descriptive to evaluative; comma would weaken the rhetorical pivot (passes FHB-9.8-1 individually)
• "nor will there be again" — modal "shall" → "will" per FHB-9.10-1

verses.fhb_text: Before them fire devours; behind them a flame burns. The land before them is like the garden of Eden, and behind them a desolate wilderness; nothing escapes them.
verses.decision_notes: Dual locust/army imagery preserved; semicolons join the antithetical clauses naturally per FHB-9.4-2.

verses.fhb_text: Their appearance is like the appearance of horses; like war horses they run.
verses.decision_notes: Verbatim simile preserved; "run" works for both locust swarms and cavalry, sustaining FHB-5.11-1 dual imagery.

verses.fhb_text: Like the rumbling of chariots they leap over the mountaintops, like the crackling of a flame of fire devouring stubble, like a mighty army arrayed for battle.
verses.decision_notes: Three-fold simile chain; "leap" preserves the Hebrew yeraqedun and reads both as locusts skipping and chariots leaping per FHB-5.11-1.

verses.fhb_text: Before them the peoples writhe in anguish; every face turns pale.
verses.decision_notes: "every face" (distributive sense) per FHB-7.13-1 — individual faces grow pale; "writhe in anguish" preserves visceral force of yachilu per FHB-7.10-1.

verses.fhb_text: Like warriors they charge, like soldiers they scale the walls. Each marches straight ahead; they do not swerve from their paths.
verses.decision_notes: Action verbs "charge" and "scale" carry both locust and military readings per FHB-7.8-1 + FHB-5.11-1.

verses.fhb_text: They do not jostle one another; each marches on his own course. They burst through the weapons and are not halted.
verses.decision_notes: Dual imagery sustained: "burst through the weapons" works for both an undeterrable locust swarm and a disciplined army.

verses.fhb_text: They rush on the city, they run on the walls, they climb up into the houses; like a thief they enter through the windows.
verses.decision_notes: Four parallel motion verbs preserved per FHB-7.8-1; the thief simile is the prophet's own concession to invasion-imagery alongside locust-swarm imagery.

verses.fhb_text: Before them the earth quakes; the heavens tremble. The sun and the moon grow dark, and the stars withdraw their shining.
verses.decision_notes: Cosmic-disturbance language preserved; the same imagery returns at v.31 as the Day of our Father's wider eschatological frame.

verses.fhb_text: Then our Father utters his voice before his army, for his camp is exceedingly great, and mighty is the one who carries out his word. Great is the day of our Father, and very terrible. Who can endure it?
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Father is the named commander of the day-of-YHWH army — the load-bearing verse for Person identification across the chapter.

Renderings:
• "Then our Father utters his voice before his army" — YHWH → our Father (narrator naming the Person who has been speaking from v.1); waw-consecutive opened with "Then" for sequential narrative per FHB-9.3-1
• "the day of our Father" — second occurrence in the chapter; path continuity from v.1
• "Who can endure it?" — rendered as its own sentence; rhetorical question carries weight without an em dash

Hebrew/Greek: v'YHWH natan kolo lifnei cheilo (and-YHWH gave his-voice before his-army) → "Then our Father utters his voice before his army"

verses.fhb_text: "Yet even now," declares our Father, "return to me with all your heart, with fasting, with weeping, and with mourning."
verses.decision_notes: Father speaks; n'um YHWH → "declares our Father"; first-person "return to me" preserved.
verses.footnote: The Father himself is calling. "Return to me with all your heart" is not a demand for ritual but our Father's longing for his children's hearts to come home.

verses.fhb_text: Rend your hearts and not your garments. Return to YHWH your Father, for he is gracious and merciful, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love; and he relents from sending calamity.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Covenant-formula naming preserved alongside the Father's character (Ex 34:6-7 echo).

Renderings:
• "YHWH your Father" — YHWH+Elohim couplet (shuvu el-YHWH eloheikhem) preserves YHWH transliterated and resolves Elohim relationally per the covenant-formula treatment in FHB-1.2-1; prophet is speaker addressing the people, so "your Father"
• "gracious and merciful, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love" — Ex 34:6-7 character formula preserved
• "he relents from sending calamity" — niham al-hara'ah preserved

Hebrew/Greek: shuvu el-YHWH eloheikhem (return to YHWH your-God) → "Return to YHWH your Father"

verses.fhb_text: Who knows? He may turn and relent, and leave behind a blessing — a grain offering and a drink offering for YHWH your Father.
verses.decision_notes: Em dash marks the apposition (the blessing IS the grain/drink offering); colon would also work but em dash earns its place. "YHWH your Father" — same covenant-formula treatment as v.13.

verses.fhb_text: Blow a shofar in Zion! Consecrate a fast, call a sacred assembly.
verses.decision_notes: The shofar imperative carries an exclamation as in v.1; the subsequent liturgical imperatives are less urgent and read with commas.

verses.fhb_text: Gather the people; consecrate the assembly; assemble the elders; gather the children, even nursing infants. Let the bridegroom come out from his chamber, and the bride from her bridal tent.
verses.decision_notes: Four parallel imperatives joined by semicolons per FHB-9.4-2; the bride-bridegroom exception underscores that no Israelite is exempt from the assembly.

verses.fhb_text: Between the vestibule and the altar let the priests, the ministers of our Father, weep. Let them say, 'Spare your people, our Father, and do not make your inheritance an object of scorn, a byword among the nations. Why should they say among the peoples, "Where is their God?"'
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Speaker-context exception applied within a Father-speech chapter — the nations' question stays generic "God," while the priests' vocative resolves relationally.

Renderings:
• "ministers of our Father" — narrator naming; mesharetei YHWH → ministers of our Father
• "Spare your people, our Father" — priests address the Father vocatively, on behalf of the community; "O" dropped for modern naturalness per FHB-9.9-1
• "Where is their God?" — speaker-context exception: the nations' taunt uses Elohim generically and non-relationally, so it is preserved as "their God" per FHB-1.2-1 speaker-context clause

verses.fhb_text: Then our Father became jealous for his land and had pity on his people.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The hinge verse of the chapter — the Father's heart turns from judgment toward restoration.

Renderings:
• "Then our Father became jealous for his land" — vayqane YHWH → narrator-voice Father identification; waw-consecutive opened with "Then" per FHB-9.3-1
• "had pity on his people" — vayachmol al-ammo preserves the protective compassion sense; complemented by the racham theme of the Father's heart turning

verses.fhb_text: Our Father answered and said to his people: "Behold, I am sending you grain, new wine, and oil, and you will be satisfied with them, and I will no longer make you a reproach among the nations."
verses.decision_notes:
Renderings:
• "Our Father answered and said to his people" — vaya'an YHWH vayomer l'ammo, narrator-voice
• "new wine" — tirosh per FHB-4.5.1-1 (not yayin / asis); the wine-word distinction preserved

verses.fhb_text: I will remove the northern army far from you and drive it into a parched and desolate land, its vanguard into the eastern sea, and its rear guard into the western sea. Its stench will rise, and its foul smell will ascend, for it has done great things.
verses.decision_notes: Father speaks in first person; "the northern army" (hatzefoni) preserves the prophet's term; the ironic "for it has done great things" stands as its own clause without em-dash flourish.

verses.fhb_text: Fear not, O land; be glad and rejoice, for our Father has done great things!
verses.decision_notes: Mirror reversal of v.20's "it has done great things" — now the same higdil la'asot is rightly ascribed to our Father; exclamation earns its place as a genuine cry of joy.

verses.fhb_text: Fear not, you beasts of the field, for the pastures of the wilderness are green, for the tree bears its fruit; the fig tree and the vine give their full yield.
verses.decision_notes: Creation-blessing language preserved; the fear-not address extends from people (v.21) to beasts — covenant restoration reaches the whole land.

verses.fhb_text: Be glad, O children of Zion, and rejoice in YHWH your Father, for he has given you the early rain for your vindication. He has poured down for you abundant rain, the early and the latter rain, as before.
verses.decision_notes:
Renderings:
• "children of Zion" — bnei Tziyon rendered with warm familial language per FHB-5.10-1 (not "sons" alone)
• "YHWH your Father" — covenant-formula couplet preserved as in v.13

verses.fhb_text: The threshing floors will be full of grain; the vats will overflow with new wine and oil.
verses.decision_notes: "new wine" — tirosh per FHB-4.5.1-1; "shall" → "will" per FHB-9.10-1.

verses.fhb_text: I will restore to you the years that the swarming locust has eaten — the hopper, the destroyer, and the cutter, my great army that I sent among you.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Four locust kinds preserved per FHB-5.11-1; Father claims the locust swarm as "my great army."

Renderings:
• "swarming locust ... hopper, destroyer, cutter" — arbeh, yeleq, chasil, gazam preserved as four kinds per FHB-5.11-1; the dual locust/army imagery sealed by Father's own "my great army"
• Em dash introduces the appositive list of locust kinds — earns its place per FHB-9.8-1

Hebrew/Greek: ha'arbeh, ha-yeleq, he-chasil, ha-gazam — four kinds of locust, preserved

verses.fhb_text: You will eat in plenty and be satisfied, and praise the name of YHWH your Father, who has dealt wondrously with you. And my people will never again be put to shame.
verses.decision_notes: "YHWH your Father" — Father speaks naming himself by covenant identity to be praised by the people (FHB-1.2-1 v4.3 "your Father" form); "put to shame" for yevoshu per FHB-7.10-1 bosh rendering.
verses.footnote: In our Father's voice, his people are named as his children — the family name his heart has always carried.

verses.fhb_text: You will know that I am in the midst of Israel, and that I am YHWH your Father, and there is no other. And my people will never again be put to shame.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Covenant-formula self-revelation — the load-bearing verse of the chapter for the FHB-1.2-1 v2.4 clause.

Renderings:
• "I am YHWH your Father" — ani YHWH eloheikhem rendered per the explicit FHB-1.2-1 covenant-formula clause: preserve YHWH transliterated, resolve Elohim to the relational Father
• "there is no other" — v'ein od preserved as flat assertion of unique covenant identity
• "my people will never again be put to shame" — bosh per FHB-7.10-1

Hebrew/Greek: ani YHWH eloheikhem v'ein od (I-am YHWH your-God and-no other) → "I am YHWH your Father, and there is no other"

verses.fhb_text: And it will come to pass afterward that I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh: your sons and your daughters will prophesy; your old men will dream dreams; your young men will see visions.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Father speaks; "my Spirit" preserved verbatim per FHB-1.2-7 (don't pad the speaker's own naming).

Renderings:
• "I will pour out my Spirit" — Father is speaker; ruchi rendered "my Spirit" per FHB-1.2-7 (A) speaker-identifies clause; do NOT substitute "the Holy Spirit" in body — the Father's own naming stands
• "sons and daughters / old men / young men" — preserve generational scope of the outpouring; this is the filial framework Peter will quote at Pentecost (Acts 2:17)
• Colon introduces the three parallel prophetic gifts

Hebrew/Greek: eshpoch et-ruchi al-kol-basar (I-will-pour-out my-Spirit on-all-flesh) → "I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh"
verses.footnote: Our Father pours out his Spirit — the Holy Spirit — on his own sons and daughters; the Father, the Spirit, and the children are family.

verses.fhb_text: Even on the male and female servants, in those days I will pour out my Spirit.
verses.decision_notes: "my Spirit" preserved per FHB-1.2-7 (speaker identifies); the outpouring extends without social distinction.

verses.fhb_text: I will show wonders in the heavens and on the earth: blood and fire and columns of smoke.
verses.decision_notes: "the heavens" (plural in Hebrew, hashamayim) preserved per FHB-7.9-1; colon introduces the threefold sign-list.

verses.fhb_text: The sun will be turned to darkness, and the moon to blood, before the great and awesome day of our Father comes.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Borderline Day-of-YHWH verse — dominant chapter context governs.

Renderings:
• "day of our Father" — per FHB-1.2-1 explicit guidance on Joel 2:31: render per dominant chapter context (Father is the named speaker of vv.11-12, 19, 25, 27-29); path continuity from vv.1 and 11
• Cosmic-disturbance language preserved verbatim — Peter cites this verse at Pentecost (Acts 2:20) for the eschatological day to come

verses.fhb_text: And it will come to pass that everyone who calls on the name of Jesus will be saved. For in Mount Zion and in Jerusalem there will be those who escape, as our Father has said, and among the survivors will be those whom our Father calls.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Bible-Clarifies-Bible — Romans 10:13 applies the calling-on-the-name directly to Jesus, while the prophetic word and the calling itself stay with the Father.

Renderings:
• "calls on the name of Jesus" — first YHWH in the verse resolves to Jesus per FHB-1.2-1 Resolution Order step 2 + the explicit example in the rule ("Joel 2:32 [Romans 10:13 explicitly applies the verse to Jesus] → 'calls on the name of Jesus'")
• "as our Father has said" — second YHWH refers to the prophetic speaker who has been speaking through Joel; that speaker is the Father (cf. vv.12, 27)
• "those whom our Father calls" — third YHWH refers to the Father's call of the survivors; the called-ones are the Father's elect remnant

Hebrew/Greek: kol asher yikra b'shem YHWH yimalet (everyone who-calls in-name-of YHWH will-escape) → "everyone who calls on the name of Jesus will be saved"
verses.footnote: Paul applies this verse directly to Jesus — "everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved" (Romans 10:13). The name we call on for rescue is Jesus, our Father's open invitation home.

chapter_notes: Joel 2 is the load-bearing Day-of-YHWH chapter where the Father is the named commander of the army (v.11), the named speaker of the call to return (vv.12, 19, 27-29), and the named giver of the Spirit (vv.28-29). Person identification is resolved by FHB-1.2-1's Resolution Order with the Father as the dominant speaker throughout; the one exception is v.32, where FHB-1.2-1 (with FHB-1.2-2 Bible-Clarifies-Bible via Romans 10:13) resolves the first YHWH to Jesus while the subsequent two YHWH references stay with the Father. The locust army dual imagery is preserved per FHB-5.11-1 (action verbs that work for both insects and soldiers; four locust kinds named in v.25). Covenant-formula naming "YHWH your Father" appears at vv.13, 14, 23, 26, 27 — preserving the Hebrew YHWH transliteration paired with the relational Father per the v2.4 covenant clause. Two footnotes for the chapter: day_of_our_father at v.1 (first canon occurrence) and calls_on_name_of_jesus at v.32 (first canon occurrence). Wine word tirosh consistently rendered "new wine" per FHB-4.5.1-1. Three em dashes in the chapter (vv.2, 14, 25), each earning its place individually per FHB-9.8-1.

lint_flags.detail: Footnote contains Hebrew/Greek term 'YHWH'. FHB footnotes are reader-only — no Hebrew/Greek transliterations (FHB-8.4 footnote style). Move technical terms to decision_notes.
